interpretations.interpretation: A straightforward reading that this is part of a sacred genealogy: each man “begot” the next, so Aram → Amminadab → Nahshon → Salmon is understood as literal ancestral succession recorded from Israel’s history.

A legitimizing function for the larger narrative: listeners may view the passage as establishing proper descent of an important figure later in the genealogy, grounding authority or status through ancestry rather than through argument.
A covenant/heritage emphasis: the audience may interpret these named ancestors as links in the chain of God’s longstanding dealings with Israel—proof that the story is rooted in Israel’s real past.

An association with well-known figures of Judah’s story: Nahshon, for example, could evoke the tribal leadership context (Judah’s prominence), so the audience might connect the genealogy with identifiable roles within Israel’s tribal life.

A sense of fulfillment of Scripture and expectations: early readers of biblical genealogies could see the listing of names as preparing for promises that will be realized in the later individual named in the genealogy (i.e., that God’s plan proceeds through these lines).

A textual/traditional perspective: because genealogies were transmitted and read as authoritative tradition, audiences might treat the excerpt as reliable summary of established family records rather than as invention or metaphor.
